sentences.text: In a sense, unofficially, yes.
Everyone knew who I was.
And the guards knew, the commandant knew me.
And they sort of had a kind of a consideration for me and sort of, maybe, even a bit of respect.
In time, I was allowed to go to other camps by bicycle, with the car, with Tadek, to treat inmates in need of a dentist.
And so I found my brother.
Well, we were going to this particular camp that was just open, not far from us.
And as we passed on the road, I noticed a bit of clothing, a sweater on one of the inmates that I sort of spotted that some-- that I remember.
And looking closer, I could see from his body build and his physiognomy that it is my brother.
And I said to the guard, this is my brother.
And he said-- and, and some of the inmates were also from Dobra.
And they started to look at me.
And I, I pointed the finger not to say anything.
Because they also had guards, and I was afraid that those guards may arrest me and arrest even the guard that came with me.
Because this was not allowed officially.
It was just that the commandant was tolerant, and he said, OK, go out.
But you know, this is not allowed.
If somebody catch me-- catch you, I don't know anything about it.
And so I passed by, and then I couldn't because Joe raised his eyes, and he saw me.
I called my brother Joe.
And I knew that he's recognized me.
And I stopped with Tadek, and I said, Tadek, I want to go over.
This is my brother.
I want to see him, I want to say hello.
I want to ask about my mother and my sister.
And he says, wait here.
He walked over to the guards.
And he had a conversation with them.
Whatever the conversation was, they allowed him to have me come over.
They allowed me to come over and just for a few minutes say hello to my brother.
And I walked over.
And I just exchanged some words with him, and knew him-- knowing that I can't stay long.
And I told him that I'll try to come back.
And maybe, he said, he will also speak with his guards.
Maybe they will allow him to go out of the line during lunchtime.
And we can sit a little bit, not far away, in the forest, and talk.
And so I did for a few days, as long as he worked there.
And then some day, when I drove by with my bicycle and the guard, they weren't there anymore.
He was transferred somewhere else.
What I found out is that the same Krusche that wanted to end my life in Steineck was his commandant.
And when he saw his name, Jakubowicz, he said, do you have a brother.
There-- it was in Steineck.
He said, yeah.
What was his name?
He said, Branek.
He said, that swine.
I will still kill him when I see him somewhere.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And so I was afraid to go into the camp to treat anybody.
Because this would be too dangerous.
I never went into the camp.
I just came to the point where Joe worked and spent some time with Joe and then went back to the camp.
Not doing anything, really.
Zosia was coming very much every weekend practically, every second weekend to Gutenbrunn.
And we continue on our [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very, very loving relationship.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she offered to hide me when she heard that they are going to close all the labor camps and deport the inmates to concentration camps.
She begged me.
Her father came with her once and begged me.

But I just could not see myself cooped up in a basement, fear-- with fear of every moment when I hear soldiers go by, or army people go by, or people talk-- that I will be discovered, in which case they, too, would be punished.
But it was a supreme sacrifice that they offered me.
And it-- I was very grateful for it.
And I just could not see my father and myself survive under these conditions.
I was still-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's worth remembering that I was still not so bad off as most of the inmates were.
I still had some extra bread, and I still had some extra food that she brought me.
And I was still getting an extra soup, because I was the dentist.
And I still had an extra privilege to go out of camp.
So you know, altogether it wasn't something that was so pressing.
It was so final for me, going to another camp.
But little did I know that we were to be transported to Auschwitz.
When was the last time you saw Zosia?
I saw Zosia at about the beginning of August, perhaps as late as the 10th of August.
[SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In our last meeting, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] she offered to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] never give up her love for me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I didn't know [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what my life is going to have ahead of me.
I couldn't make that kind of a promise.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But she was an important aspect of my survival, which I should always remember her.
Well, all of the labor camps in the Poznan area were liquidated.
The Soviets were coming into Poland.
And the threat of being found by the Soviets was something that the Germans could not risk.
And so we were marched to a town called Gleiwitz that was German.
That was already Germany.
It was German before 1939.
We walked to Gleiwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Perhaps four, five, six hours.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I take it back.
We did not walk to Gleiwitz.
We walked to a railroad station, at which there were cars waiting for us.
And there we were packed into 100, 120 to a car.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from there, we were transported over the rails left and right, wherever there was a free rail-- because the army was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had precedence, and they were shoveling them to the east and back past to the east and west.
And we were just kept on some spots on dead rails for days.
Pardon?
We are received once or twice, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] minimum amount of food and very rarely coffee.
Only water with the food, but very, very little.
A lot of the inmates who survived two years died on this trip.
It was a torture.
Because there wasn't enough room to stand, not enough room to sit.
And whoever sat down couldn't get up.
And oftentimes, at each station that we came, we had to get rid of these-- those that had died.
And the SS just said to us, just toss them over the wagon, just toss them over the edge of the train.
And so whoever wasn't completely dead was also dead by the time he landed.
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] Many of the inmates lost their minds and were shot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when civilians were trying to bring us some water some time at the station, they were being driven away by the SS.
And oftentimes we passed passenger cars with dining rooms and looked at their lives and thought how different one who is not born a Jew, who is born a German, can live, even during the war.
It was such a discrepancy.
It was such a hard thing to realize, that misery was only for us, not for everyone.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] as the rails were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] free, we were brought closer and closer to Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And with each joint of the rail, I'd lay there and hear the voice-- doom, death, doom, death, doom, death, doom, death.
I thought I'd lose my mind.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I looked at my brothers in the wagon, my father, and said to myself, don't they know?
This is doom.
This is death.
I know, the rails told me that.
And so on the fifth day-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] dirty, unwashed, hungry, depleted of strength-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] our wagons stopped.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was dawn.
We looked outside, and we saw [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that where-- we are at a camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were glad.
At least our life will continue as it did in Gutenbrunn, as it did in Steineck, miserable as it was.
But some of us will continue living.
And then soon we saw as the daylight broke, people in striped suits, in zebra suits and striped hats of the same material.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we didn't know who they were.
Because we were still wearing civilian clothes in the labor camps.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one of them signaled to us, by putting his hand over his neck and sliding it across, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which could only mean nothing good is awaiting us, that our life is over.
And then as we were slowly pushed forwards by the cars, by the locomotive, we saw others.
And they raised their hands up in the air, to show strength.
And so we took their signal.
And we knew that nothing good is awaiting us.
But this may be our survival.
And soon, the car stopped.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the doors slid open.
And chaos broke-- the SS men with their dogs, with their whips, yelling, [SPEAKING GERMAN], leave everything on the rails.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I left everything that I had-- my little satchel.
But I kept my dental instruments.
I thought they had kept me alive so long.
Perhaps they will have a chance to keep me alive again, here.
But an SS man came over.
And he pushed his whip at-- at me.
And he said, what is in there.
And I told him those are dental instruments.
He said, [GERMAN], leave behind.
And I said, those are dental instruments.
I use them in camp.
And he just tore them off my shoulder and slid them across the rails.
The little box broke up, and all the instruments lay there.
And I looked at them, and I said, this is the end of me.
Without these, I will never survive.
Nor will my father.
Beg your pardon.
My father who was with me.
Yes, nor will my father.
My brother was not with me, just my father.
But I understood that my brother is on the same transport, also coming from the other camp to Auschwitz with the same transport.
It was a huge transport, perhaps as many as 100 cars.
And then we were asked to walk forwards and undress.
And we undressed, and we came in front of a group of SS men.
One of them was obviously the reigning person, and he stood nonchalantly.
And we walked, five of us in front of him, naked as we were, taking in some air to show some chest, trying to look strong, rubbing our cheeks with our fists, so that they will look pink.
Trying to stretch out, so we wouldn't have such small behind.
And he just mimicked.
Some time he turned his eyes.
Some time he turned his head.
Some time he just touched something.
They knew what it meant.
This was well-rehearsed and used many, many times.
And they, according to his whim and to his decision, ordered people to the left or right.
And it soon became evident that those that going to the left were elderly people, people that did not look like they would be the good group, so to speak, the strong ones.
And then when it came to my father, they pointed him to the left and me to the right.
They asked him what he did.
And he said he was a farmer.
Sounded good-- couldn't say merchant, because that was a bad label for a Jew.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I stood behind them, my father on the other side.
And then one of the next groups tried to escape, and perhaps deliberately so, that he could be shot.
And pandemonium broke out.
And I looked and saw an occasion and I went over and I grabbed my father by his sleeve, by his arm, and pulled him over to me.
And I can't understand, but for some reason they didn't notice it.
And my father was able to come with me along, in the group that was designated to enter Auschwitz.
Yes, I ultimately found out when I was working in the [GERMAN] that his name was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dr.
Mengele, Josef Mengele.
He came to, to select people from the [GERMAN] house.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were brought to a hall where we were deloused.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we didn't know what the next hall has for us.
Because it said [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] means shower.
But we knew that the showers were not exactly showers.
They were very often used to [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Zyklon-B people to death, to poison them--
--with cyanide.
We knew that.
We knew, we heard, in Gutenbrunn, as we heard about Treblinka, we heard about Auschwitz.
We knew what Auschwitz is all about.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when the doors slammed closed, I closed my eyes.
And I thought, this is it.
This is not going to be a shower.
Can you imagine how I felt, when the first drops of water fell on my head, and I stuck my tongue out, and I saw it was water?
It was like a new life again, even in concentration camp.
Life was so precious.
You didn't want to lose it, not in this god forsaken place.
And so we found Joe in the showers.
And we, the three of us, did-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] made up our mind to stay close to one another.
So when they will partition off groups to here and there, we would stay together possibly.
And so we got-- when we were tattooed with numbers on our arm--
Yes I do.
Yes.
The number is 141129.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father's number, we kept him in the middle, was 141130.
And my brother's number was 141131.
So he was in back, I was in front, and my father in the middle.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were given the same zebra style, zebra stripe uniforms, as we have seen the others wear.
And we were brought into a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what was called a [GERMAN], death block.
Why that block, I don't know.
But it was a quarantine block.
If any one of us would have had typhoid or any kind of infectious disease, we would all go to the chimney.
Fortunately, no one did.
And so after three weeks or so, IG farm people came and picked us to work in Auschwitz III, which was Buna, in an [INAUDIBLE] camp of Auschwitz III, by the name of Furstengrube.
Was this main camp, the Stammlager.
This was Auschwitz main camp.
The very main camp where we were delivered, with the sign, 'Arbeit macht frei' and with the orchestra playing and with the double fences and with the, with the electric wire.
And we were told, as the barbers were shearing our hair off, that here you don't walk, you run.
And if you don't like it, you can go to the fence and end it there.
There was a sign to that, proclaiming that.
You have arrived in Auschwitz, which is a concentration camp.
You're going to be working here.
And if you are strong, you may survive three months.
And if you are very strong, six months.
And if you don't like it, you can go to the fence and end it now.
Needless to say, I didn't have this in my mind.
I wanted to survive.
I wanted to go on, as long as I could, at least.
And so we were transported from Auschwitz-- after three weeks in this death block, quarantine block-- to Furstengrube.
We walked.
It was only about six, seven hours of walk.
It was near Magdeburg, in Harz, in-- no, this was in Furstengrube.
It was not far from main camp Auschwitz and not far from Buna.
There we were sent to the coal mine, where we were to dig coal after-- at night, the foremen were blasting off the coal off the walls.
We were to pick up the coal and put it on lorries, transporting lorries, out.
No, no, I had no instruments.
And I have not-- no one of us was employed.
We were waiting to either be exterminated or be assigned to some kind of work camp.
Oh yes, we did, we did, we did.
We had contact with Auschwitz inmates that were there for a year, year and a half.
Difficult to believe, but some of them survived, mostly non-Jews.
They were treated differently as we were.
No non-Jew was ever gassed in a chamber.
They had to be accused of something, in order to, to lose his life.
Jews were the victims, just for being Jewish.
In Furstengrube?
We were sent to the coal mine.
And each of us was assigned to a certain foreman.
And the foreman was assigning us to work.
My father and I were assigned to a foreman.
And he brought us into a small little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] area, where coal was just that night sprung off the walls, dynamited.
And we had to bring this coal out and put it outside on a roll-- a lorry, which was on a railroad, small railroad, rails.
And my brother was assigned to move these lorries to the end of the, of the coal mine.
And that's how they brought them to the surface.

And there they were put on big wagons and driven to Buna, where they were producing synthetic rubber from coal, extracting the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] properties from coal to create the synthetic rubber needed for the war-- tires and et cetera.
Well, this was a complete different life.
It was torture at work.
Because we had to bend down-- it was only about three feet high-- carry these heavy blocks of coal.
Our hands became braised and our faces were covered with the dust of coal.
And when we came back to the camp, it was hard to find a bit of water.
Because there was only a little pipe with little spouts, tiny little holes, where you could catch a few, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] few-- half a liter of water and put it on your face.
And that wasn't enough to clean off this thick dust.
So we soon looked like real [GERMAN], like real coal miners, dark in their faces.
Our hands calloused, bleeding.
I did not think that I could survive.

Until one day, Richard Grimm, who was the Lageralteste after Kurt Goldberg lost his position in Gutenbrunn-- who was a mensch, who was a real decent man-- he [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was a friend of mine.
And he saw me in the washroom.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He says, look at you.
What do you look like?
He became the Lageralteste in Furstengrube.
I just wanted to speak with the Lagerfuhrer, because he wants to open a dental station, and recommended you to become the dentist here, to be manning the dental station.
But look at you?
How could you?
You are just a Muselmann.
Muselmann was the expression for someone who is almost finished.
We have to stop now.
But we'll continue with the next tape shortly.
